Ben Carter: From vuln-triage: no crypto exploit; looks like timing/config race.
Priya Singh: Enable audit events: idp.assertion.missing_role and scim.deprov.fail with tenant_id and request_id. Need data for postmortem.

title: #eng-security: 4132110000
preview:
Priya Desai: Heads up — we're scheduling retirement of the Legacy Token (v1) used by older mobile SDKs and a handful of partners. Need a short design for a compatibility bridge (shim), telemetry plan, and an escape hatch. Cutting without this will cause auth failures.
Alex Kim: How big is the surface? tenants + traf...
text:
Priya Desai: Heads up — we're scheduling retirement of the Legacy Token (v1) used by older mobile SDKs and a handful of partners. Need a short design for a compatibility bridge (shim), telemetry plan, and an escape hatch. Cutting without this will cause auth failures.
Alex Kim: How big is the surface? tenants + traffic numbers?
Priya Desai: Rough numbers: ~12 partner tenants, ~3 mobile apps on SDK <=3.2.1. Peak auth calls ~30k/day; steady ~8–10k/day.
Mateo Alvarez: Proposal: run an auth-bridge sidecar at the edge that accepts the old token, performs a limited exchange to a v2 bearer (server-side) and injects Authorization: Bearer <v2>. Keeps core services unchanged. Alternative: map headers in the proxy but sidecar gives better telemetry and rate-limiting.
Jen Harris: Security notes: the bridge must validate signature+claims, add TTL, and emit audit events (legacy_token=true). We cann...

title: #eng-security: 2117770001
preview:
Security Ops: FYI team — rolling out a mandatory deprecation of reusable service API keys. Any key that is reused across >1 service or has TTL >24h will be disabled after a 10-day remediation window. See actions below. :warning:

Ethan Park: Quick: does this hit keys in shared vault paths or only plaintext in config...
text:
Security Ops: FYI team — rolling out a mandatory deprecation of reusable service API keys. Any key that is reused across >1 service or has TTL >24h will be disabled after a 10-day remediation window. See actions below. :warning:

Ethan Park: Quick: does this hit keys in shared vault paths or only plaintext in configs?

Security Ops: Both. Shared vault paths are OK if the secret is automatically rotated to <=24h or issued per-service. Plaintext or duplicated keys in configs are in-scope and must be replaced with per-service short-lived creds or OIDC.

QA Infra: Our test harness uses a shared token across environments. Can we get an exemption?

Security Ops: Temporary exceptions allowed but need a ticket with justification and mitigation (IP allowlist, restricted network, expiration date). File in #eng-sre with tag "shortlived-exception". Exceptions reviewed within 48h.

SRE oncall: Mig...


```diff
text:
Alice Chen: heads-up, vendor published GPU stack advisories (driver + cuDNN). initial CVEs called out: CVE-2026-0421 (driver privilege escalation) and CVE-2026-0422 (cuDNN malformed tensor OOB). NCCL had a separate infoleak fix in 2.17.0 (CVE-2026-0550).
Marco Diaz: paste the link? need to map affected builds and our images.
Alice Chen: https://vendor.example/nvidia/advisory/2026-0421-0422 - drivers 535.86+ and cuDNN 9.2.x affected per vendor.
Priya Nair: TL;DR: high local exploitability. For hosted multitenant nodes this is HIGH (processes run as root on GPU nodes). Dedicated/private less urgent but still P1 for compliance.
Ben Carter: prioritization suggestion: Hosted: 48h emergency patch (canary -> stage -> prod). Dedicated: 30-day advisory + scheduling. Private: publish patch bundles + compatibility notes.
sec-oncall: Also ensure audit logging captures driver/package installs and ...

title: #eng-security: 1779004321
preview:
Ava Chen: Quick question — if we deprovision a user via SCIM (Okta), do we strip email addresses from service logs immediately? We're getting a privacy request and need to know whether logs still contain user emails. cc @Ravi Patel @Maya Ghosh
Ravi Patel: short answer: deprovisioning removes account but logs are ret...
text:
Ava Chen: Quick question — if we deprovision a user via SCIM (Okta), do we strip email addresses from service logs immediately? We're getting a privacy request and need to know whether logs still contain user emails. cc @Ravi Patel @Maya Ghosh
Ravi Patel: short answer: deprovisioning removes account but logs are retained per retention policy. we don't automatically redact fields in historical logs unless a deletion job runs. :frowning:
Lin Ortega: do we have a list of which pipelines ingest raw usernames/emails? I think api-gateway and embeddings-service still log `user_email` in request metadata right now.
SecurityBot: incoming event: `SCIM: user_deactivated` for user alice@example.com at 2026-02-28T14:12:09Z — no automatic log purge scheduled.
Maya Ghosh: for this specific privacy request can we run a targeted redaction? if yes, what's the process — ticket + legal signoff?
Ava Chen:...
```

text:
Marcus Lin: Dropping proposed marketplace security claims here for final accuracy check before Docs publishes + PMM puts into AWS/GCP listing. Please nitpick wording.

Claims (draft):
1) “All customer data is encrypted in transit (TLS 1.2+) and at rest (AES‑256).”
2) “Customer content (prompts/outputs) is not used to train models.”
3) “Hosted API request/response payloads are retained for 30 days by default for abuse monitoring and debugging; customers can request shorter retention.”
4) “Audit logs are available for admin actions and API key events, and can be exported to customer SIEM.”
5) “Keys are managed via cloud KMS; Dedicated/Private customers can use customer-managed keys.”
6) “Data residency: processing occurs in the region selected by the customer (where available).”

Need to confirm what’s true across Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private, and what needs qualifiers.

Naomi Feldman...

text:
Hanae Suzuki: heads up: getting more field questions on audit log exports (esp. Aurora + Summit). can we sanity check what we *actually* support today + what fields we include? they’re asking for “SIEM integration”, “near real-time”, “JSON schema”, “who/what/when” etc.

Marcus Lin: ok, current state (pls correct if i miss anything):
- Audit logs exist for Console + API actions (auth, key mgmt, org/user/admin actions, model deployment changes, policy changes, export config changes)

title: #eng-security: 1767873008
preview:
Monica Patel: looping in #eng-security for review on SDK parity work: auth env var naming + precedence + secret handling/redaction. We want one standard across Python/TS/Go.

Ibrahim Khan: Proposed env vars (new + canonical):
- `REDWOOD_API_KEY`
- `REDWOOD_BEARER_TOKEN` (for service-to-service tokens)
Legacy vars to...
text:
Monica Patel: looping in #eng-security for review on SDK parity work: auth env var naming + precedence + secret handling/redaction. We want one standard across Python/TS/Go.

Ibrahim Khan: Proposed env vars (new + canonical):
- `REDWOOD_API_KEY`
- `REDWOOD_BEARER_TOKEN` (for service-to-service tokens)
Legacy vars to deprecate but still read for a bit: `RW_API_KEY`, `REDWOOD_TOKEN`, `REDWOOD_KEY` (varies by SDK today)
Precedence proposal: explicit client param > env vars (canonical first, then legacy) > error.
Also: ensure we never log secrets, even in debug.

Marcus Lin: thx. couple initial reactions:
1) naming: +1 on `REDWOOD_API_KEY`. For token, prefer `REDWOOD_ACCESS_TOKEN` over `REDWOOD_BEARER_TOKEN` (people copy/paste “Bearer …” into env vars and we end up double-prefixing).
2) precedence: agree explicit > env. If multiple env vars are set, we need deterministic selection AND a w...

title: #eng-security: 1760784422
preview:
Fatima Noor: Got a Q from Helios Defense on today’s call: can they use *their* PKI root / customer-signed bundles instead of trusting a Redwood signing cert? They’re saying policy prohibits installing anything unless it chains to their internal root.

Marcus Lin: Ugh, yeah, we’ve heard variants of this. They’re basi...
text:
Fatima Noor: Got a Q from Helios Defense on today’s call: can they use *their* PKI root / customer-signed bundles instead of trusting a Redwood signing cert? They’re saying policy prohibits installing anything unless it chains to their internal root.

Marcus Lin: Ugh, yeah, we’ve heard variants of this. They’re basically asking for customer-controlled trust root or delegation. Do they mean “we verify Redwood signature AND also we sign internally after receipt” or literally “Redwood ships unsigned and customer signs”? Big difference.

Fatima Noor: They want the installer to accept bundles that are signed by them (their offline release team) because the artifact has to go through their internal approval/signing gateway before it can cross into the air-gap.

Connor O'Brien: From release side: if we allow arbitrary customer signatures, we’re effectively letting them bypass Redwood provena...

title: #eng-security: 1760648201
preview:
Marcus Lin: circling back on break-glass wording. we need something Support/SE can copy/paste + something we can point auditors to. current draft is too hand-wavy.

Kevin Osei: +1. also need to be super explicit on what gets logged locally when break-glass is used. auditors will ask “prove you can detect/trace excep...
text:
Marcus Lin: circling back on break-glass wording. we need something Support/SE can copy/paste + something we can point auditors to. current draft is too hand-wavy.

Kevin Osei: +1. also need to be super explicit on what gets logged locally when break-glass is used. auditors will ask “prove you can detect/trace exceptions”.

Connor O'Brien: fwiw from release side: we should treat break-glass as “installer bypass”, not a release process bypass. release artifacts still signed; break-glass is only for install edge cases.

Ethan Park: implementation wise we can require a flag + a “reason” string and optionally ticket ID. but policy wants minimum required fields so we can enforce.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: the language should be: only for time-bound emergency situations, approved by Security, and produces an immutable local audit record. and it must be an explicit opt-in (no env var magic).

Olga ...

2) the inline policy for installer role has `ec2:*` in one spot (I know it’s narrowed later, but this is too broad for customer environments)

title: #eng-security: 1747515520
preview:

Marcus Lin: +1. Needs to be concrete + defensible in audits. Also needs to be implem...
text:
Hanae Suzuki: We need to lock down rollback criteria for KMS rotation cutover (dual-read -> new-only) before we publish the runbook. Can we align on hard thresholds + signals? I keep seeing “roll back if things look bad” in drafts.

Marcus Lin: +1. Needs to be concrete + defensible in audits. Also needs to be implementable on-call.

Kevin Osei: I can propose signals based on what we’re already instrumenting in the wrapper: `kms.error_category`, `kms.provider_error_code`, `kms.request_latency_ms`, plus decrypt mismatch counters from the dual-read path.

Sean Gallagher: SRE angle: we need “rollback triggers” that are (a) measurable in dashboards (ENG-4828), (b) fast to act on (flip flag), and (c) minimize blast radius (avoid retry storms).

Ethan Park: Control-plane wise, cutover is driven by the config flag set (the same thing we discussed in the dual-read thread). Rollback is basicall...

text:
Marcus Lin: Kicking off privacy review for PLG signal routing → HubSpot. Need to confirm exactly what fields we’re pushing, retention, and who can see it. @Dr. Aisha Rahman @Michael Grant @Olga Petrov looping you in.

Marcus Lin: Current draft: signal scoring service writes `plg_signal` records to analytics table + pushes a subset to HubSpot Company properties + a timeline note + a task. Question: any blockers on what constitutes “personal data” here?

Logan Wright: From eng side: we can keep it workspace/org-level only. No individual user identifiers needed. The pipeline currently joins on `workspace_id` → `hubspot_company_id` via billing/account mapping.

Dr. Aisha Rahman: Good starting point. Let’s be explicit: HubSpot is a CRM and broadly accessible in GTM. Treat anything we send there as “high visibility.” We should avoid raw telemetry, raw URLs, IPs, user agents, individual emai...

text:
Marcus Lin: Kicking off review for Private min-pack log policy + redaction. Can someone paste the current structured fields we’re planning to ship (control plane + data plane)? Main question from Sec: are we logging any request payload/prompt/completion at all?

Omar El-Sayed: For runtime (data plane) we’re aligning to the structured fields from pr-18521. Current plan is (high level): `timestamp, level, service, component, request_id, trace_id, span_id, route, model, status_code, error_type, latency_ms, input_tokens, output_tokens, total_tokens, batch_id (optional), kv_cache_hit (bool), gpu_id (optional), pod, node`. Plus `tenant_id_hash` (not raw tenant) and `client_app` if provided.

Naomi Feldman: Thanks. Need to be super explicit: do we log *any* prompt text, completion text, tool/function args, or request bodies? Even “debug logs” sometimes sneak this in.

Omar El-Sayed: Default ...

text:
Kevin Osei: Kicking off error taxonomy alignment for the KMS wrapper. Goal: same categories + log fields across Hosted/Dedicated/Private so on-call + dashboards are consistent.

Kevin Osei: Proposal (canonical `kms.error_category`):
- `AccessDenied` (incl IAM/policy)
- `NotFound` (key/alias not found)
- `KeyDisabled` (disabled / pending deletion)
- `Throttling` (rate exceeded)
- `Timeout` (client deadline)
- `InvalidCiphertext` (decrypt failure / bad blob)
- `InvalidRequest` (bad params, wrong region, malformed ARN)
- `ServiceUnavailable` (5xx, network)
- `Unknown`

Kevin Osei: Retryability suggestion:
- retry: `Throttling`, `Timeout`, `ServiceUnavailable`
- don’t retry: `AccessDenied`, `NotFound`, `KeyDisabled`, `InvalidCiphertext`, `InvalidRequest`

Kevin Osei: Log fields (structured):
- `dep.name=kms` (per observability standard)
- `kms.provider` (aws|gcp|azure)
- `kms.operation` (...

title: #eng-security: 1743532107
preview:

text:
Marcus Lin: hey folks — pulling together audit log reqs for the model metadata + changelog project. we’ve got enterprise customers asking “who changed what/when” for model version promos + metadata edits.

Need alignment on:
1) audit event types we must emit (create/update/promote/deprecate/rollback?)
2) what fields must be captured (actor, diff, etc.)
3) retention expectations (hosted vs dedicated vs private)
4) SIEM export requirements

Looping in @Naomi Feldman @Kevin Osei @Olga Petrov @Svetlana Petrenko + @Michael Nguyen @Ethan Park

Naomi Feldman: +1, this is coming up in procurement language now ("change management", "immutable audit trail"). we should define a minimum bar for GA and a higher bar for regulated/Private.

title: #eng-security: 1740745509

text:
Marcus Lin: quick q on legal hold + “break-glass” process for retention controls. We need to lock down: who can place a hold, who approves, and how we audit it end-to-end. Trying to avoid us hand-waving in security reviews.

Sofia Mendes: +1. From Legal perspective we need: (a) clear authority to place/extend/release holds, (b) a written reason/case ID, (c) audit trail that’s exportable, and (d) safeguards so engineers can’t silently hold/preserve data without approval.

Kevin Osei: on implementation side, we can add a `hold` object to policy evaluation that overrides TTL enforcement. But I need exact semantics: does hold apply per tenant, per data class, per region? and do we allow “incident hold” separate from “legal hold”?

Dr. Aisha Rahman: we should split:
- Legal hold (external/legal request)
- Incident hold (internal, time-bounded for IR/debug)
Both must be audited. Incident ho...

title: #eng-security: 1740496409
preview:

Olga Petrov: +1 thanks for pulling this together. For SOC 2 we need evidence both that (a) controls exist by desi...
text:
Marcus Lin: hey folks — quick alignment needed w/ GRC on SOC2 evidence for RBAC v2 work. what artifacts do you need from us (design docs, tests, logs, change records)? trying to avoid scramble in April.

Olga Petrov: +1 thanks for pulling this together. For SOC 2 we need evidence both that (a) controls exist by design and (b) they’re operating effectively over the audit period. For RBAC v2 specifically it maps primarily to logical access + change mgmt + logging/monitoring.

Daniel Carter: If it helps, auditors usually ask in 3 buckets:
1) Design: policies/standards + system description
2) Implementation: screenshots / configs showing it’s actually in place
3) Operating effectiveness: samples over time (tickets, PRs, logs, access reviews)

Marcus Lin: ok. can you be more specific for RBAC v2? like exact docs / sample types.

Olga Petrov: yep. Here’s the “minimum set” we should plan for...

text:
Marcus Lin: Need quick alignment on terminology for the retention controls docs + policy schema. We keep mixing “audit logs” and “request logs” in PRDs + tickets.

Marcus Lin: Specifically: what are the data classes we’re committing to in v1 retention enforcement, and what do we call them so it survives security reviews?

Naomi Feldman: +1. I’m seeing “inference logs”, “API logs”, “request/response logs” used interchangeably. For enterprise questionnaires they ask “Do you retain customer prompts/completions” and “Do you have audit logs”. Those are NOT the same thing.

Kevin Osei: From implementation POV (ENG-18420/33/34): we have two concrete stores in v1:
- request log store (per request metadata, status, latency, token counts, etc; optional payload logging)
- audit log store (control plane events, authz/admin actions)

Kevin Osei: Everything else (traces/metrics) is in observability...

preview:

text:
Kevin Osei: Need quick alignment for GRC / compliance questionnaires on “retention vs export” semantics for SIEM-ready audit export beta.

Kevin Osei: Specifically: when asked “how long do you retain audit logs” vs “does SIEM export retain them” and “what happens if destination is down for N days” — what’s the approved language?

Marcus Lin: +1. We’ve got people conflating export with retention. Export is a delivery mechanism, not a retention guarantee.

Olga Petrov: Yep. For SOC2/ISO evidence responses we need consistent phrasing.

Olga Petrov: Proposed structure:
1) Redwood audit log retention (source-of-truth)
2) Export delivery semantics (at-least-once, retries, dedup)
3) Customer responsibilities (SIEM retention, config, access)
4) Deletion semantics (customer copies are customer-controlled)

Kira Thompson: Do we want to cite specific numbers in Slack, or “per contract / plan” la...

Marcus Lin: We need to lock down the break-glass flow for RBAC v2 this week. Goal: time-bound elevation + loud alerting + evidence. Can we align on (1) who can request, (2) who approves, (3) how we enforce TTL + auto-revoke, (4) what gets logged/alerted?

David Kim: From IT side: we need a clean workflow for internal admins + on-call. Please don’t make it “edit a DB row by hand” :grimacing:

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Current proposal in design doc: create a temporary role binding (subject -> role=BreakGlassAdmin) scoped to org/project/env, with explicit `expires_at` and `reason`. Enforced by authz lib (deny if now > expires_at) + background sweeper to delete/disable expired bindings.

text:
Marcus Lin: Kicking off the naming convention thread. We need something consistent before we lock the permission registry + start annotating endpoints. Current state is a mess (some `org.admin`, some `manage_keys`, some hardcoded strings in gateway).

Marcus Lin: Proposals I’ve heard:
- `org.api_keys.rotate`
- `controlplane.api_keys.rotate`
- `redwood.org.api_keys.rotate`
- `rbac:org:api_keys:rotate` (pls no)

Marcus Lin: Main questions:
1) separator (`.` vs `:`)
2) namespacing (product/service vs domain)
3) versioning (in string vs metadata)
4) verb set (create/update/delete/manage?)

Jordan Lee: +1. From policy engine side I strongly prefer `.` separated, lowercase, deterministic segments. Easy to lint + match prefixes.

Jordan Lee: I’d vote for: `<domain>.<resource>.<action>` where domain = customer-facing conceptual area (org/project/deployment/audit/etc), NOT internal service nam...

title: #eng-security: 1738605400
preview:
Marcus Lin: Need a crisp call for the backup/restore toolkit: do we *ever* allow backup artifacts to include secret material (k8s Secrets / DB passwords / API keys), or do we strictly do references only (Vault path, Secrets Manager ARN, etc.)?

Marcus Lin: Context: installer `backup create` is generating manifest + ...
text:
Marcus Lin: Need a crisp call for the backup/restore toolkit: do we *ever* allow backup artifacts to include secret material (k8s Secrets / DB passwords / API keys), or do we strictly do references only (Vault path, Secrets Manager ARN, etc.)?

Marcus Lin: Context: installer `backup create` is generating manifest + encrypted blob. If we include raw secrets we’re basically creating a new secret store + retention problem. If we *don’t* include them, restores will fail unless customer has their secret system ready.

Hanae Suzuki: Strong preference: backups should NOT contain secret values. Only references + checks (e.g., “expected secret name X exists”, maybe hash of public cert, etc.).

Hanae Suzuki: For k8s secrets specifically, we can record:
- secret name + namespace
- expected keys (not values)
- owner resource
Then `restore apply` can validate + prompt operator to rehydrate.

Conno...

text:
Marcus Lin: looping in on usage insights export + shareable reports. before we flip beta on for any enterprise accounts, need answers on: (1) who can see cost (2) who can export (3) retention + data residency constraints (4) audit log events/fields. can someone drop current plan?

Dylan Brooks: yep. current implementation sketch: insights page behind feature flag + existing cost permission. export is async job -> signed URL. we have "workspace setting: hide cost" concept in console already but not sure it’s enforced in this path yet.

Naomi Feldman: from product/security reqs: cost visibility must be gated by role AND workspace setting. if workspace hides cost, nobody (except maybe workspace owner?) should be able to view/export. we need to confirm desired override semantics.
